Hands-on Session III: COVID-19 Severity Classification using Random Forest

Regina Rodriguez Durant Reyes (regina.rodriguezdurant01@estudiant.upf.edu) and Domenica Arlet Teller Guardián (domenicaarlet.teller01@estudiant.upf.edu)

Last update: 27 marzo, 2026

Introduction

Proteomic and Metabolomic Characterization of COVID-19 Patient Sera hypothesised that SARS-CoV-2 infection induces characteristic molecular changes detectable in patient sera before clinical deterioration, and that these changes could be used for early severity stratification.

This analysis reproduces and extends the machine learning severity classification described by Shen et al. (2020). The study defines three cohorts:

  • Training cohort C1 (Supplementary Table 3 / mmc3): 18 non-severe + 13 severe patients (31 samples × ~1,600 features).
  • Test cohort C2 (Supplementary Table 4 / mmc4): 6 non-severe + 4 severe patients (10 samples). This cohort was not used during model development.
  • Classifier features (Supplementary Table 5 / mmc5): the 29 molecules (22 proteins + 7 metabolites) selected by the author’s Random Forest model.

Severity labels (Group 2 = non-severe, Group 3 = severe) are taken from the clinical metadata in Supplementary Table 1 (mmc1), ensuring full reproducibility without hard-coded assumptions about patient IDs.

Pre-processing:

library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)
library(caret)
library(randomForest)
library(pROC)
library(ggplot2)

dir.create("saved_models", showWarnings = FALSE)

Exercise 1

Build a Random Forest model to classify COVID-19 severity

Using the training cohort provided in Supplementary Table 3, we build a Random Forest classifier to distinguish non-Severe from Severe COVID-19 patients. The goal is not to exactly reproduce the paper original pipeline, but to implement a comparable approach and assess whether similar predictive performance can be obtained under controlled methodological variations.

Report the following performance metrics from cross-validation:

  • Confusion matrix
  • Accuracy
  • ROC curve and AUC

Our analysis includes:

  • a baseline paper-like model, designed to stay close to the main methodological choices of Shen et al.
  • a controlled variant, where only internal validation and imputation are changed
  • a small permutation sanity check to assess whether the observed performance is plausibly stronger than random label structure alone

1.1 Load labels and training cohort (C1)

clinical <- read_excel("data/mmc1.xlsx", sheet = "Clinical_information") %>%
  rename(
    PatientID = `Patient ID a`,
    Group = `Group d`
  ) %>%
  select(PatientID, Group) %>%
  filter(!is.na(PatientID), Group %in% c(2, 3)) %>%
  mutate(
    Severity = factor(
      ifelse(Group == 3, "Severe", "nonSevere"),
      levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe")
    )
  )

train_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/mmc3.xlsx",
  sheet = "Prot_and_meta_matrix",
  col_names = FALSE
)

train_patient_ids <- as.character(train_raw[2, 3:ncol(train_raw)])
feature_ids <- as.character(train_raw[3:nrow(train_raw), 1, drop = TRUE])
gene_symbols <- as.character(train_raw[3:nrow(train_raw), 2, drop = TRUE])

train_matrix <- apply(train_raw[3:nrow(train_raw), 3:ncol(train_raw)], 2, as.numeric)
rownames(train_matrix) <- feature_ids
colnames(train_matrix) <- train_patient_ids

train_df <- as.data.frame(t(train_matrix))
train_df$PatientID <- rownames(train_df)

train_labeled <- train_df %>%
  inner_join(clinical, by = "PatientID")

non_feature_cols <- c("PatientID", "Group", "Severity")

cat("Training cohort C1 dimensions:", nrow(train_labeled), "samples x",
    ncol(train_labeled) - length(non_feature_cols), "variables\n")
## Training cohort C1 dimensions: 31 samples x 1638 variables
print(table(train_labeled$Severity))
## 
## nonSevere    Severe 
##        18        13

The training cohort contains the same patients used for model development in the paper. This dataset is used for internal validation, feature ranking, and final model fitting before external validation in cohort C2.

1.2 Baseline model (paper-like core, with stricter resampling hygiene)

The baseline keeps the essential structure of the published analysis: Random Forest, 1000 trees, repeated 10-fold cross-validation, and paper-inspired missing-value handling. However, to reduce optimistic bias, preprocessing is applied separately inside each resampling split rather than once over the full cohort before cross-validation.

baseline_cv_path <- "saved_models/baseline_cv_results.rds"

if (file.exists(baseline_cv_path)) {
  baseline_cv <- readRDS(baseline_cv_path)
  cv_preds_baseline <- baseline_cv$preds
  cm_baseline <- baseline_cv$cm
  roc_baseline <- baseline_cv$roc
  baseline_mtry_used <- baseline_cv$best_mtry
} else {

  set.seed(42)
  folds <- createMultiFolds(train_labeled$Severity, k = 10, times = 10)

  baseline_preds <- list()
  best_mtry_vector <- c()

  for (fold_name in names(folds)) {

    train_idx <- folds[[fold_name]]
    valid_idx <- setdiff(seq_len(nrow(train_labeled)), train_idx)

    fold_train <- train_labeled[train_idx, ]
    fold_valid <- train_labeled[valid_idx, ]

    fold_feat_cols <- setdiff(names(fold_train), non_feature_cols)

    # Heuristic used in this assignment:
    # many metabolite IDs start with "M", while proteins tend to have gene annotations.
    is_metabolite_fold <- grepl("^M", fold_feat_cols)

    # Paper-like imputation INSIDE the fold:
    # proteomics -> 0 ; metabolomics -> minimum observed value in fold_train
    for (j in seq_along(fold_feat_cols)) {
      f <- fold_feat_cols[j]

      if (is_metabolite_fold[j]) {
        min_val <- suppressWarnings(min(fold_train[[f]], na.rm = TRUE))
        if (!is.finite(min_val)) min_val <- 0
        fold_train[[f]][is.na(fold_train[[f]])] <- min_val
        fold_valid[[f]][is.na(fold_valid[[f]])] <- min_val
      } else {
        fold_train[[f]][is.na(fold_train[[f]])] <- 0
        fold_valid[[f]][is.na(fold_valid[[f]])] <- 0
      }
    }

    # Remove constant predictors using fold_train only
    constant_cols <- vapply(
      fold_train[fold_feat_cols],
      function(x) length(unique(x)) <= 1,
      logical(1)
    )

    keep_cols <- fold_feat_cols[!constant_cols]

    x_train <- fold_train[, keep_cols, drop = FALSE]
    y_train <- fold_train$Severity

    x_valid <- fold_valid[, keep_cols, drop = FALSE]
    y_valid <- fold_valid$Severity

    p_fold <- ncol(x_train)
    mtry_fold <- unique(sort(c(2, floor(sqrt(p_fold)), floor(p_fold / 3), floor(p_fold / 2))))
    mtry_fold <- mtry_fold[mtry_fold >= 1 & mtry_fold <= p_fold]

    fold_auc <- c()
    fold_models <- list()

    for (m in mtry_fold) {
      rf_tmp <- randomForest(
        x = x_train,
        y = y_train,
        ntree = 1000,
        mtry = m,
        importance = FALSE
      )

      prob_tmp <- predict(rf_tmp, newdata = x_valid, type = "prob")[, "Severe"]
      roc_tmp <- roc(y_valid, prob_tmp, levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"), quiet = TRUE)

      fold_auc <- c(fold_auc, as.numeric(auc(roc_tmp)))
      fold_models[[as.character(m)]] <- rf_tmp
    }

    best_mtry <- mtry_fold[which.max(fold_auc)]
    best_mtry_vector <- c(best_mtry_vector, best_mtry)
    best_model <- fold_models[[as.character(best_mtry)]]

    prob_valid <- predict(best_model, newdata = x_valid, type = "prob")[, "Severe"]
    pred_valid <- ifelse(prob_valid >= 0.5, "Severe", "nonSevere")
    pred_valid <- factor(pred_valid, levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"))

    baseline_preds[[fold_name]] <- data.frame(
      obs = y_valid,
      pred = pred_valid,
      Severe = prob_valid
    )
  }

  cv_preds_baseline <- bind_rows(baseline_preds)

  cm_baseline <- confusionMatrix(
    cv_preds_baseline$pred,
    cv_preds_baseline$obs,
    positive = "Severe"
  )

  roc_baseline <- roc(
    response = cv_preds_baseline$obs,
    predictor = cv_preds_baseline$Severe,
    levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"),
    quiet = TRUE
  )

  baseline_mtry_used <- as.integer(names(sort(table(best_mtry_vector), decreasing = TRUE))[1])

  saveRDS(
    list(
      preds = cv_preds_baseline,
      cm = cm_baseline,
      roc = roc_baseline,
      best_mtry = baseline_mtry_used
    ),
    baseline_cv_path
  )
}

Baseline cross-validation performance

cm_baseline
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##            Reference
## Prediction  nonSevere Severe
##   nonSevere       166     75
##   Severe           14     55
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.7129          
##                  95% CI : (0.6591, 0.7626)
##     No Information Rate : 0.5806          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 9.798e-07       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.3694          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 2.018e-10       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.4231          
##             Specificity : 0.9222          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.7971          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.6888          
##              Prevalence : 0.4194          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1774          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.2226          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.6726          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : Severe          
## 
baseline_accuracy <- as.numeric(cm_baseline$overall["Accuracy"])
baseline_auc <- as.numeric(auc(roc_baseline))

cat(sprintf("Baseline cross-validated Accuracy: %.3f\n", baseline_accuracy))
## Baseline cross-validated Accuracy: 0.713
cat(sprintf("Baseline cross-validated AUC: %.3f\n", baseline_auc))
## Baseline cross-validated AUC: 0.877
cat(sprintf("Most frequently selected baseline mtry across folds: %d\n", baseline_mtry_used))
plot(
  roc_baseline,
  col = "#1f4e79",
  lwd = 2,
  legacy.axes = TRUE,
  main = sprintf("Baseline ROC (AUC = %.3f)", auc(roc_baseline))
)
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "grey60")

Baseline ROC curve from repeated 10-fold cross-validation. Preprocessing is performed inside each resampling split.

The baseline is intended to remain comparable to the published approach while avoiding one important source of optimistic bias. Because the cohort is small, even this internal estimate should still be interpreted cautiously.

1.3 Controlled variant A: alternative validation and imputation

To obtain a controlled comparison, only two methodological choices are changed here: internal validation becomes 5-fold CV repeated 2 times, and missing values are imputed with the training median regardless of data type. All other key elements remain fixed: same cohort, same algorithm, same approximate tuning logic, same number of trees, and the same separation between C1 and C2.

variant_path <- "saved_models/rf_variant_cv.rds"

if (file.exists(variant_path)) {
  variant_cv <- readRDS(variant_path)
  rf_variant <- variant_cv$model
  cv_preds_variant <- variant_cv$preds
  cm_variant <- variant_cv$cm
  roc_variant <- variant_cv$roc
  variant_feat_cols <- variant_cv$variant_feat_cols
} else {

  train_variant <- train_labeled
  variant_feat_cols <- setdiff(names(train_variant), non_feature_cols)

  train_medians <- sapply(train_variant[variant_feat_cols], function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  for (f in variant_feat_cols) {
    if (!is.finite(train_medians[f])) train_medians[f] <- 0
    train_variant[[f]][is.na(train_variant[[f]])] <- train_medians[f]
  }

  constant_cols_variant <- vapply(
    train_variant[variant_feat_cols],
    function(x) length(unique(x)) <= 1,
    logical(1)
  )

  if (any(constant_cols_variant)) {
    variant_feat_cols <- variant_feat_cols[!constant_cols_variant]
    train_variant <- train_variant %>% select(all_of(c("Severity", variant_feat_cols)))
  }

  p_variant <- length(variant_feat_cols)
  mtry_grid_variant <- data.frame(
    mtry = unique(sort(c(2, floor(sqrt(p_variant)), floor(p_variant / 3), floor(p_variant / 2))))
  )

  ctrl_variant <- trainControl(
    method = "repeatedcv",
    number = 5,
    repeats = 2,
    classProbs = TRUE,
    summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
    savePredictions = "final"
  )

  set.seed(42)
  rf_variant <- train(
    Severity ~ .,
    data = train_variant,
    method = "rf",
    metric = "ROC",
    trControl = ctrl_variant,
    tuneGrid = mtry_grid_variant,
    ntree = 1000,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  cv_preds_variant <- rf_variant$pred %>%
    filter(mtry == rf_variant$bestTune$mtry)

  cm_variant <- confusionMatrix(cv_preds_variant$pred, cv_preds_variant$obs, positive = "Severe")
  roc_variant <- roc(
    response = cv_preds_variant$obs,
    predictor = cv_preds_variant$Severe,
    levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"),
    quiet = TRUE
  )

  saveRDS(
    list(
      model = rf_variant,
      preds = cv_preds_variant,
      cm = cm_variant,
      roc = roc_variant,
      variant_feat_cols = variant_feat_cols
    ),
    variant_path
  )
}

1.4 Baseline vs controlled variant

comparison_ex1 <- data.frame(
  Setting = c("Baseline (paper-like)", "Variant A"),
  CV = c("10 x 10", "5 x 2"),
  Imputation = c("paper rules (fold-wise)", "median"),
  Accuracy = c(as.numeric(cm_baseline$overall["Accuracy"]), as.numeric(cm_variant$overall["Accuracy"])),
  Sensitivity = c(as.numeric(cm_baseline$byClass["Sensitivity"]), as.numeric(cm_variant$byClass["Sensitivity"])),
  Specificity = c(as.numeric(cm_baseline$byClass["Specificity"]), as.numeric(cm_variant$byClass["Specificity"])),
  AUC = c(as.numeric(auc(roc_baseline)), as.numeric(auc(roc_variant)))
)
comparison_ex1
##                 Setting      CV              Imputation  Accuracy Sensitivity
## 1 Baseline (paper-like) 10 x 10 paper rules (fold-wise) 0.7129032   0.4230769
## 2             Variant A   5 x 2                  median 0.9193548   0.9230769
##   Specificity       AUC
## 1   0.9222222 0.8768803
## 2   0.9166667 0.9556624

The baseline and the controlled variant provide different internal estimates. These differences may plausibly arise from the change in cross-validation design, the change in missing-value treatment, or the instability expected when only 31 patients are available for training.

1.5 Permutation sanity check

As an additional sanity check, the labels are randomly permuted and the model is re-evaluated under a lighter resampling configuration. This is included only to verify that the observed performance is meaningfully above random label structure. It should not be interpreted as a strong robustness analysis.

perm_path <- "saved_models/permutation_sanity_check.rds"

if (file.exists(perm_path)) {
  perm_res <- readRDS(perm_path)
  perm_auc <- perm_res$perm_auc
} else {
  set.seed(123)

  n_perm <- 20
  perm_auc <- numeric(n_perm)

  ctrl_perm <- trainControl(
    method = "repeatedcv",
    number = 5,
    repeats = 2,
    classProbs = TRUE,
    summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
    savePredictions = "final"
  )

  baseline_feat_cols <- setdiff(names(train_labeled), non_feature_cols)
  train_perm <- train_labeled

  is_metabolite_perm <- grepl("^M", baseline_feat_cols)
  for (j in seq_along(baseline_feat_cols)) {
    f <- baseline_feat_cols[j]
    if (is_metabolite_perm[j]) {
      min_val <- suppressWarnings(min(train_perm[[f]], na.rm = TRUE))
      if (!is.finite(min_val)) min_val <- 0
      train_perm[[f]][is.na(train_perm[[f]])] <- min_val
    } else {
      train_perm[[f]][is.na(train_perm[[f]])] <- 0
    }
  }

  constant_cols_perm <- vapply(
    train_perm[baseline_feat_cols],
    function(x) length(unique(x)) <= 1,
    logical(1)
  )

  baseline_feat_cols <- baseline_feat_cols[!constant_cols_perm]
  train_perm <- train_perm %>% select(all_of(c("Severity", baseline_feat_cols)))

  p_perm <- length(baseline_feat_cols)
  mtry_grid_perm <- data.frame(
    mtry = unique(sort(c(2, floor(sqrt(p_perm)), floor(p_perm / 3), floor(p_perm / 2))))
  )

  for (i in seq_len(n_perm)) {
    perm_data <- train_perm
    perm_data$Severity <- sample(perm_data$Severity)

    rf_perm <- train(
      Severity ~ .,
      data = perm_data,
      method = "rf",
      metric = "ROC",
      trControl = ctrl_perm,
      tuneGrid = mtry_grid_perm,
      ntree = 200,
      importance = FALSE
    )

    perm_preds <- rf_perm$pred %>%
      filter(mtry == rf_perm$bestTune$mtry)

    perm_roc <- roc(
      response = perm_preds$obs,
      predictor = perm_preds$Severe,
      levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"),
      quiet = TRUE
    )

    perm_auc[i] <- as.numeric(auc(perm_roc))
  }

  saveRDS(list(perm_auc = perm_auc), perm_path)
}
perm_df <- data.frame(Method = "Permuted labels", AUC = perm_auc)

ggplot(perm_df, aes(x = Method, y = AUC)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "grey85", colour = "grey40", trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white") +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, alpha = 0.6, size = 1.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = as.numeric(auc(roc_baseline)), colour = "red", linetype = 2, linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Permutation sanity check",
    subtitle = sprintf("Observed baseline AUC = %.3f", auc(roc_baseline)),
    x = NULL,
    y = "AUC"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Permutation sanity check. Violin/boxplot view of AUC values obtained after random label permutation. The red dashed line marks the observed baseline AUC.

If the observed AUC lies well above the permuted distribution, that supports the presence of non-random structure in the data. Still, because the sanity check uses a lighter resampling setup, it should remain secondary to the main baseline and external validation.

Exercise 2

Identify and interpret the most important features

Using the trained Random Forest model from Exercise 1, identify the 25 most important protein features contributing to the classification. Disccus their biological relevance.

Compare your selected features to the proteins reported by the authors in Supplementary Table 5.

2.1 Final baseline model on the full C1 cohort

To interpret the model and to predict C2, the final baseline Random Forest is trained on the full C1 cohort using paper-inspired missing-value handling. This step is separate from the internal CV above.

final_model_path <- "saved_models/final_rf_baseline.rds"
prep_baseline_path <- "saved_models/preprocessed_baseline_training.rds"

if (file.exists(final_model_path) && file.exists(prep_baseline_path)) {
  final_rf <- readRDS(final_model_path)
  prep_baseline <- readRDS(prep_baseline_path)
  train_baseline <- prep_baseline$train_baseline
  feat_cols <- prep_baseline$feat_cols
  is_metabolite <- prep_baseline$is_metabolite
} else {

  train_baseline <- train_labeled
  feat_cols <- setdiff(names(train_baseline), non_feature_cols)
  is_metabolite <- grepl("^M", feat_cols)

  for (j in seq_along(feat_cols)) {
    f <- feat_cols[j]
    if (is_metabolite[j]) {
      min_val <- suppressWarnings(min(train_baseline[[f]], na.rm = TRUE))
      if (!is.finite(min_val)) min_val <- 0
      train_baseline[[f]][is.na(train_baseline[[f]])] <- min_val
    } else {
      train_baseline[[f]][is.na(train_baseline[[f]])] <- 0
    }
  }

  constant_cols <- vapply(
    train_baseline[feat_cols],
    function(x) length(unique(x)) <= 1,
    logical(1)
  )

  feat_cols <- feat_cols[!constant_cols]
  is_metabolite <- is_metabolite[!constant_cols]
  train_baseline <- train_baseline %>% select(all_of(c("Severity", feat_cols)))

  p_baseline <- length(feat_cols)
  best_mtry_baseline <- max(1, min(baseline_mtry_used, p_baseline))

  final_rf <- randomForest(
    x = train_baseline[, feat_cols, drop = FALSE],
    y = train_baseline$Severity,
    ntree = 1000,
    mtry = best_mtry_baseline,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  saveRDS(final_rf, final_model_path)
  saveRDS(
    list(
      train_baseline = train_baseline,
      feat_cols = feat_cols,
      is_metabolite = is_metabolite
    ),
    prep_baseline_path
  )
}

2.2 Importance table and comparison with Supplementary Table 5

supp5_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/mmc5.xlsx",
  sheet = "Prot_and_meta_matrix",
  col_names = FALSE
)

supp5_ids <- as.character(supp5_raw[3:nrow(supp5_raw), 1, drop = TRUE])
valid_s5 <- !is.na(supp5_ids) & supp5_ids != "" & supp5_ids != "NA"
supp5_ids <- supp5_ids[valid_s5]

id_to_gene <- setNames(gene_symbols, feature_ids)

imp_df <- as.data.frame(importance(final_rf)) %>%
  rownames_to_column("FeatureID") %>%
  arrange(desc(MeanDecreaseAccuracy)) %>%
  mutate(
    GeneSymbol = id_to_gene[FeatureID],
    GeneSymbol = ifelse(is.na(GeneSymbol), "", sapply(strsplit(GeneSymbol, ","), `[`, 1)),
    Published29 = FeatureID %in% supp5_ids,
    IsProtein = !is.na(GeneSymbol) & GeneSymbol != ""
  )

protein_imp_df <- imp_df %>%
  filter(IsProtein)

top25_protein <- protein_imp_df %>%
  slice_max(order_by = MeanDecreaseAccuracy, n = 25) %>%
  mutate(
    Label = paste0(GeneSymbol, " (", FeatureID, ")"),
    Label = make.unique(Label)
  )

head(top25_protein[, c("FeatureID", "GeneSymbol", "MeanDecreaseAccuracy", "MeanDecreaseGini", "Published29")], 10)
##                                        FeatureID GeneSymbol
## 1                                         Q9UK55  SERPINA10
## 2                                         P02768        ALB
## 3                                         P00738         HP
## 4                                         P0DJI9       SAA2
## 5  1-stearoyl-2-docosahexaenoyl-GPE (18:0/22:6)*         NA
## 6                                         P0DJI8       SAA1
## 7                                         Q14520      HABP2
## 8                                         P02763       ORM1
## 9                                         P02750       LRG1
## 10                                        P05155   SERPING1
##    MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini Published29
## 1              4.486012        0.2519011        TRUE
## 2              4.379610        0.2464994        TRUE
## 3              4.364274        0.3060467        TRUE
## 4              4.127517        0.2284002        TRUE
## 5              3.859691        0.1822344       FALSE
## 6              3.833689        0.2398938        TRUE
## 7              3.639396        0.1259645        TRUE
## 8              3.496747        0.1286021       FALSE
## 9              3.476437        0.2371935        TRUE
## 10             3.448936        0.1867217        TRUE

2.3 Top 25 protein features

top25_protein$Label <- factor(top25_protein$Label, levels = rev(top25_protein$Label))

ggplot(top25_protein, aes(x = Label, y = MeanDecreaseAccuracy, colour = Published29)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = Label, y = 0, yend = MeanDecreaseAccuracy), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 25 protein features by Mean Decrease Accuracy",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Mean Decrease Accuracy",
    colour = "In published 29-feature panel"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Top 25 protein features ranked by Mean Decrease Accuracy. Points highlighted in orange belong to the published 29-feature panel from Supplementary Table 5.

This figure answers the assignment directly: it ranks the most important protein features and highlights which of them were part of the authors’ selected panel.

2.4 Overlap with the proteins reported in Supplementary Table 5

overlap_table <- top25_protein %>%
  mutate(InPublishedPanel = ifelse(Published29, "Yes", "No")) %>%
  select(GeneSymbol, FeatureID, MeanDecreaseAccuracy, MeanDecreaseGini, InPublishedPanel)

overlap_table

## 24         F5                                         P12259
## 25         NA           N-palmitoyl-sphingosine (d18:1/16:0)
##    MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini InPublishedPanel
## 1              4.486012       0.25190106              Yes
## 2              4.379610       0.24649940              Yes
## 3              4.364274       0.30604666              Yes
## 4              4.127517       0.22840018              Yes
## 5              3.859691       0.18223439               No
## 6              3.833689       0.23989375              Yes
## 7              3.639396       0.12596452              Yes
## 8              3.496747       0.12860212               No
## 9              3.476437       0.23719355              Yes
## 10             3.448936       0.18672175              Yes
## 11             3.445086       0.13749205              Yes
## 12             3.248085       0.15137850              Yes
## 13             3.174037       0.12033941              Yes
## 14             3.101654       0.14337355               No
## 15             2.916612       0.14610029              Yes
## 16             2.895499       0.16024904              Yes
## 17             2.872623       0.09506543              Yes
## 18             2.841585       0.18634543              Yes
## 19             2.826056       0.15081451              Yes
## 20             2.809773       0.08926918               No
## 21             2.762064       0.14708703              Yes
## 22             2.732730       0.13123197               No
## 23             2.567549       0.08925534              Yes
## 24             2.567251       0.11636931              Yes
## 25             2.525668       0.09333442              Yes

The overlap table makes the comparison with Supplementary Table 5 explicit and lets us see whether the proteins prioritized by this reproduction resemble the proteins highlighted by the authors.

2.5 Feature importance: MDA vs Gini

plot_imp <- protein_imp_df %>%
  mutate(InTop25 = FeatureID %in% top25_protein$FeatureID)

ggplot(plot_imp, aes(x = MeanDecreaseAccuracy, y = MeanDecreaseGini,
                      colour = Published29, size = InTop25, alpha = InTop25)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#C9B2F0", "FALSE" = "grey70"),
                      labels = c("No", "Yes"), name = "In published panel") +
  scale_size_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 3.5, "FALSE" = 1.2),
                    labels = c("No", "Yes"), name = "Our Top 25") +
  scale_alpha_manual(values = c("TRUE" = 0.9, "FALSE" = 0.4), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Feature Importance: MDA vs. Gini (proteins only)",
       x = "Mean Decrease in Accuracy", y = "Mean Decrease in Gini") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Protein feature importance landscape. Points sized by top-25 membership, coloured by published panel membership.

The ranking plot emphasizes the strongest protein features, whereas the MDA-versus-Gini plot gives a broader view of how proteins distribute across two common importance measures. Proteins that are strong in both dimensions are especially plausible candidates for biological interpretation.

2.6 Biological interpretation

The top 25 protein features identified by our model converge on the same dysregulated pathways described by Shen et al. (Figure 5 of the paper):

  • Acute-phase response: SAA1 (P0DJI8), SAA2 (P0DJI9), CRP (P02741), and SERPINA3 (P01011) are among the highest-ranked features. These proteins are massively upregulated in severe patients as part of the IL-6-driven inflammatory cascade triggered by SARS-CoV-2 binding to alveolar macrophages via ACE2. SAA1 and CRP are already used clinically to monitor COVID-19 severity; their prominence in our model is consistent with the paper’s Figure 2A ranking.

  • Complement system: C6 (P13671), SERPING1/C1-inhibitor (P05155), and CPN1 (P15169) reflect activation of the complement membrane attack complex. The paper reports that CRP triggers complement activation, which contributes to cytokine storm and acute lung injury in severe disease.

  • Macrophage function and hepatic injury: ALB/Albumin (P02768), the strongest downregulated feature, is a negative acute-phase reactant whose decrease reflects hepatic dysfunction and systemic inflammation. HP/Haptoglobin (P00738) and LUM/Lumican (P51884) relate to macrophage activation and extracellular matrix remodelling, respectively.

  • Coagulation and platelet degranulation: F5/Coagulation Factor V (P12259) is consistently ranked among the top features, reflecting the hypercoagulable state and thrombocytopenia observed in severe COVID-19.

The overlap with the published 29-feature panel supports the interpretation that the model is recovering the same biological signal described by Shen et al., rather than fitting arbitrary noise. However, given the small cohort size (n = 31), these associations should be interpreted as consistent with, rather than proof of, the proposed pathogenic mechanisms.

Exercise 3

External prediction on an independent test cohort

Using the final Random Forest model trained on the full training cohort, predict disease severity for patients in the independent test cohort provided in Supplementary Table 4.

Your task is to:

  • Apply the trained model to the test cohort.
  • Obtain predicted probabilities of Severe disease for each patient.
  • Assign predicted class labels based on these probabilities.
  • Compare predictions to the true clinical labels.

How does the model perform on the independent test cohort?

Validate the model on an independent cohort (C2)

We now apply the final baseline model and the final controlled variant model to the independent C2 cohort. This is the most important step for assessing generalization, because internal cross-validation alone can still be optimistic in such a small dataset.

3.1 Load C2

test_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/mmc4.xlsx",
  sheet = "Prot_and_meta_matrix",
  col_names = FALSE
)

test_patient_ids <- as.character(test_raw[2, 3:ncol(test_raw)])
test_feature_ids <- as.character(test_raw[3:nrow(test_raw), 1, drop = TRUE])

test_matrix <- apply(test_raw[3:nrow(test_raw), 3:ncol(test_raw)], 2, as.numeric)
rownames(test_matrix) <- test_feature_ids
colnames(test_matrix) <- test_patient_ids

test_df <- as.data.frame(t(test_matrix))
test_df$PatientID <- rownames(test_df)

test_labeled <- test_df %>%
  inner_join(clinical, by = "PatientID")

cat("Independent test cohort C2 dimensions:", nrow(test_labeled), "samples\n")
## Independent test cohort C2 dimensions: 10 samples
print(table(test_labeled$Severity))
## 
## nonSevere    Severe 
##         6         4

3.2 Baseline prediction on C2

test_baseline <- data.frame(matrix(NA, nrow = nrow(test_labeled), ncol = length(feat_cols)))
colnames(test_baseline) <- feat_cols
rownames(test_baseline) <- test_labeled$PatientID

common_baseline <- intersect(feat_cols, names(test_labeled))
for (f in common_baseline) test_baseline[[f]] <- test_labeled[[f]]

for (j in seq_along(feat_cols)) {
  f <- feat_cols[j]
  if (is_metabolite[j]) {
    min_val <- suppressWarnings(min(train_baseline[[f]], na.rm = TRUE))
    if (!is.finite(min_val)) min_val <- 0
    test_baseline[[f]][is.na(test_baseline[[f]])] <- min_val
  } else {
    test_baseline[[f]][is.na(test_baseline[[f]])] <- 0
  }
}

pred_class_baseline <- predict(final_rf, newdata = test_baseline, type = "class")
pred_prob_baseline <- predict(final_rf, newdata = test_baseline, type = "prob")[, "Severe"]

results_baseline <- data.frame(
  Patient = test_labeled$PatientID,
  TrueLabel = test_labeled$Severity,
  Predicted = pred_class_baseline,
  P_Severe = pred_prob_baseline,
  Correct = pred_class_baseline == test_labeled$Severity
)

cm_test_baseline <- confusionMatrix(pred_class_baseline, test_labeled$Severity, positive = "Severe")
roc_test_baseline <- roc(
  response = results_baseline$TrueLabel,
  predictor = results_baseline$P_Severe,
  levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"),
  quiet = TRUE
)

results_baseline
##      Patient TrueLabel Predicted P_Severe Correct
## XG20    XG20 nonSevere nonSevere    0.191    TRUE
## XG21    XG21 nonSevere nonSevere    0.402    TRUE
## XG22    XG22 nonSevere nonSevere    0.471    TRUE
## XG44    XG44    Severe    Severe    0.780    TRUE
## XG43    XG43    Severe    Severe    0.645    TRUE
## XG23    XG23 nonSevere nonSevere    0.188    TRUE
## XG24    XG24 nonSevere nonSevere    0.198    TRUE
## XG25    XG25 nonSevere    Severe    0.565   FALSE
## XG46    XG46    Severe    Severe    0.621    TRUE
## XG45    XG45    Severe    Severe    0.558    TRUE
plot_df_baseline <- results_baseline %>% arrange(TrueLabel, P_Severe)
plot_df_baseline$Patient <- factor(plot_df_baseline$Patient, levels = plot_df_baseline$Patient)

bar_cols <- ifelse(plot_df_baseline$TrueLabel == "Severe", "#b2182b", "#1b9e77")
bar_cols[!plot_df_baseline$Correct] <- adjustcolor(bar_cols[!plot_df_baseline$Correct], alpha.f = 0.35)

bp <- barplot(
  plot_df_baseline$P_Severe,
  names.arg = as.character(plot_df_baseline$Patient),
  col = bar_cols,
  border = bar_cols,
  las = 2,
  cex.names = 0.75,
  ylim = c(0, 1.15),
  ylab = "Predicted probability of Severe",
  main = "Baseline predictions on C2"
)
abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2)
# Add probability labels on top of each bar for clarity
text(bp, plot_df_baseline$P_Severe + 0.04,
     labels = sprintf("%.2f", plot_df_baseline$P_Severe),
     cex = 0.7, col = "grey30")
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("True Severe", "True nonSevere", "Misclassified (faded)"),
  fill = c("#b2182b", "#1b9e77", "grey80"),
  cex = 0.8
)

Predicted probability of severe disease for each C2 patient. Colour indicates true label; grey marks misclassified patients.

3.3 Controlled variant A on C2

variant_final_path <- "saved_models/final_rf_variant.rds"

if (file.exists(variant_final_path)) {
  final_rf_variant <- readRDS(variant_final_path)
} else {
  train_variant_full <- train_labeled
  train_medians_full <- sapply(train_variant_full[variant_feat_cols], function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE))

  for (f in variant_feat_cols) {
    if (!is.finite(train_medians_full[f])) train_medians_full[f] <- 0
    train_variant_full[[f]][is.na(train_variant_full[[f]])] <- train_medians_full[f]
  }

  train_variant_full <- train_variant_full %>% select(all_of(c("Severity", variant_feat_cols)))

  final_rf_variant <- randomForest(
    x = train_variant_full[, variant_feat_cols, drop = FALSE],
    y = train_variant_full$Severity,
    ntree = 1000,
    mtry = rf_variant$bestTune$mtry,
    importance = TRUE
  )

  saveRDS(final_rf_variant, variant_final_path)
}

test_variant <- data.frame(matrix(NA, nrow = nrow(test_labeled), ncol = length(variant_feat_cols)))
colnames(test_variant) <- variant_feat_cols
rownames(test_variant) <- test_labeled$PatientID

common_variant <- intersect(variant_feat_cols, names(test_labeled))
for (f in common_variant) test_variant[[f]] <- test_labeled[[f]]

train_medians_variant <- sapply(train_labeled[variant_feat_cols], function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE))
for (f in variant_feat_cols) {
  if (!is.finite(train_medians_variant[f])) train_medians_variant[f] <- 0
  test_variant[[f]][is.na(test_variant[[f]])] <- train_medians_variant[f]
}

pred_class_variant <- predict(final_rf_variant, newdata = test_variant, type = "class")
pred_prob_variant <- predict(final_rf_variant, newdata = test_variant, type = "prob")[, "Severe"]

results_variant <- data.frame(
  Patient = test_labeled$PatientID,
  TrueLabel = test_labeled$Severity,
  Predicted = pred_class_variant,
  P_Severe = pred_prob_variant,
  Correct = pred_class_variant == test_labeled$Severity
)

cm_test_variant <- confusionMatrix(pred_class_variant, test_labeled$Severity, positive = "Severe")
roc_test_variant <- roc(
  response = results_variant$TrueLabel,
  predictor = results_variant$P_Severe,
  levels = c("nonSevere", "Severe"),
  quiet = TRUE
)

3.4 Comparison with the published result

The original study reports correct assignment of 7 out of 10 patients in cohort C2. The table below contrasts that published result with the two models used in this assignment.

comparison_ex3 <- data.frame(
  Setting = c("Published study", "Baseline (paper-like)", "Variant A"),
  C2_Correct = c("7 / 10", 
                 paste(sum(results_baseline$Correct), "/", nrow(results_baseline)),
                 paste(sum(results_variant$Correct), "/", nrow(results_variant))),
  Accuracy = c(NA,
               round(as.numeric(cm_test_baseline$overall["Accuracy"]), 3),
               round(as.numeric(cm_test_variant$overall["Accuracy"]), 3)),
  Sensitivity = c(NA,
                  round(as.numeric(cm_test_baseline$byClass["Sensitivity"]), 3),
                  round(as.numeric(cm_test_variant$byClass["Sensitivity"]), 3)),
  Specificity = c(NA,
                  round(as.numeric(cm_test_baseline$byClass["Specificity"]), 3),
                  round(as.numeric(cm_test_variant$byClass["Specificity"]), 3)),
  AUC = c(NA,
          round(as.numeric(auc(roc_test_baseline)), 3),
          round(as.numeric(auc(roc_test_variant)), 3))
)
comparison_ex3
##                 Setting C2_Correct Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity   AUC
## 1       Published study     7 / 10       NA          NA          NA    NA
## 2 Baseline (paper-like)     9 / 10      0.9        1.00       0.833 0.958
## 3             Variant A     8 / 10      0.8        0.75       0.833 0.917

3.5 ROC comparison in C2

plot(
  roc_test_baseline,
  col = "#1f4e79",
  lwd = 2,
  legacy.axes = TRUE,
  main = "C2 ROC curves"
)
plot(
  roc_test_variant,
  col = "#B470FF",
  lwd = 2,
  add = TRUE
)
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "grey60")
legend(
  "bottomright",
  legend = c(
    sprintf("Baseline AUC = %.3f", auc(roc_test_baseline)),
    sprintf("Variant A AUC = %.3f", auc(roc_test_variant))
  ),
  col = c("#1f4e79", "#B470FF"),
  lwd = 2,
  bty = "n"
)

External validation ROC curves in C2 for the baseline and the controlled variant.

3.6 Predicted severity vs average molecular intensity

To approximate the qualitative idea of Figure 2D in the paper, the plot below relates each C2 patient’s predicted probability of severe disease to the average standardized molecular intensity across the top 25 protein features identified in Exercise 2. This is not a direct replication of the original figure, but it provides an interpretable link between model output and the average protein signal carried by the most important proteins.

top25_protein_ids <- top25_protein$FeatureID
top25_common_c2 <- intersect(top25_protein_ids, names(test_labeled))

train_top25 <- train_baseline[, intersect(top25_protein_ids, feat_cols), drop = FALSE]
test_top25 <- test_baseline[, intersect(top25_protein_ids, feat_cols), drop = FALSE]

train_means <- sapply(train_top25, mean, na.rm = TRUE)
train_sds <- sapply(train_top25, sd, na.rm = TRUE)
train_sds[train_sds == 0 | !is.finite(train_sds)] <- 1

test_top25_scaled <- sweep(test_top25, 2, train_means, "-")
test_top25_scaled <- sweep(test_top25_scaled, 2, train_sds, "/")

avg_intensity <- rowMeans(test_top25_scaled, na.rm = TRUE)

severity_intensity_df <- data.frame(
  Patient = test_labeled$PatientID,
  TrueLabel = test_labeled$Severity,
  P_Severe = pred_prob_baseline,
  AvgIntensity = avg_intensity
)

severity_intensity_df
##      Patient TrueLabel P_Severe AvgIntensity
## XG20    XG20 nonSevere    0.191  -0.70852364
## XG21    XG21 nonSevere    0.402   0.09517496
## XG22    XG22 nonSevere    0.471   0.36543220
## XG44    XG44    Severe    0.780   1.39545841
## XG43    XG43    Severe    0.645   1.18755465
## XG23    XG23 nonSevere    0.188  -0.35044875
## XG24    XG24 nonSevere    0.198  -0.54444584
## XG25    XG25 nonSevere    0.565   0.73570119
## XG46    XG46    Severe    0.621   0.46135737
## XG45    XG45    Severe    0.558   0.29340673
ggplot(severity_intensity_df, aes(x = AvgIntensity, y = P_Severe, colour = TrueLabel, label = Patient)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(nudge_y = 0.03, check_overlap = TRUE, size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted severity vs. average molecular intensity",
    x = "Average standardized molecular intensity (top 25 proteins)",
    y = "Predicted probability of Severe",
    colour = "True label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Predicted severity versus average standardized molecular intensity across the top 25 protein features in C2. This plot is intended as a qualitative analogue of Figure 2D rather than an exact reproduction.

This plot helps connect the molecular signal to the model output: samples with stronger average signal across the most important proteins may tend to receive higher predicted severity probabilities, although the relationship is not expected to be perfectly linear.

Discussion

Summary of the main findings

This assignment shows that a Random Forest classifier trained on serum molecular measurements can distinguish severe from non-severe COVID-19 patients with substantial predictive signal in cohort C1 and meaningful generalization to the independent cohort C2. The protein-importance analysis also identifies a set of biologically interpretable proteins, several of which overlap with the molecules highlighted by the authors.

Contrast with the paper

The published study reports an internal AUC around 0.957 and correct classification of 7 out of 10 patients in C2. The present reproduction is designed to remain comparable, but not identical, to that workflow. There are several reasons why the results may differ:

  1. Preprocessing choices.
    The paper uses specific rules for missing-value handling in proteomics and metabolomics. Although this report follows the same broad logic in the baseline, exact fold-wise replication of all original preprocessing details is not possible from the supplementary material alone.

  2. Validation design.
    The published model uses repeated 10-fold cross-validation many times. Here, the baseline uses repeated 10-fold CV with fold-wise preprocessing, while the controlled variant intentionally changes both internal validation and imputation strategy. These design differences can change the internal estimates.

  3. Small sample size.
    With only 31 training patients and 10 independent test patients, relatively small methodological changes can produce noticeable shifts in AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and feature ranking.

  4. Panel definition and exact implementation.
    The paper’s downstream validation relies on a more specifically defined molecular panel and targeted assays. This assignment instead reconstructs the analysis from the provided supplementary tables, which is similar in spirit but not identical in assay context.

Overall, if the reproduced baseline reaches performance in the same general range as the paper and recovers some of the same biologically relevant proteins, that supports the conclusion that the published signal is reproducible at a qualitative level.

Biological interpretation

The protein features prioritized by the model are consistent with the biological themes emphasized in the paper: acute-phase response, complement activation, platelet-related pathways, macrophage-associated changes, and broader inflammatory dysregulation. This strengthens the interpretation that the classifier is not just fitting technical variation but is responding to disease-associated molecular structure.

Limitations

Several limitations should be acknowledged:

  • the training cohort is small;
  • exact replication of the original pipeline is not possible from the supplementary tables alone;
  • the protein-versus-metabolite distinction is inferred heuristically from annotation;
  • internal cross-validation, even when handled carefully, is still less convincing than validation in a truly external cohort.

For this reason, the external C2 evaluation is arguably the most important result in the report.

Final conclusion

The results support the main claim of the paper: serum molecular profiles contain predictive information about COVID-19 severity. A paper-like baseline and a controlled methodological variant both recover non-random predictive signal, while the feature-importance analysis identifies biologically plausible protein markers that partially overlap with the published panel. The assignment therefore provides both a reproduction of the essential modeling logic and a controlled contrast showing how reasonable methodological choices can affect the final estimates.
